summary:
The La Hoguette culture, named after a site in Normandy, France, and dated to approximately 6200 to 5600 BCE, represents an early and unusual element of the Neolithic transition in western Europe. It is characterized by a distinctive type of pointed-base pottery with bone-tempered fabric, and is found in a broad arc from the Rhine valley westward into France and Switzerland. Its origins and relationship to other Neolithic traditions have been debated, with some researchers suggesting connections to early Mediterranean Neolithic groups related to the [[event:1422|Cardial Pottery culture]], while others propose it represents independent ceramic adoption by indigenous Mesolithic hunter-gatherer communities.

The La Hoguette phenomenon is significant for raising the question of how the Neolithic transition operated at the frontier between the expanding farming world and the indigenous hunter-gatherers of western Europe. The pottery style appears in some contexts associated with hunter-gatherer subsistence rather than full farming economies, suggesting that ceramics could spread independently of the full agricultural package through contact and exchange between farming and foraging communities. This complicates the simple model of farming advancing as a unified front and illustrates the complex, multi-faceted nature of the Neolithic transition in Europe.

The La Hoguette culture is relatively poorly understood compared to better-documented contemporaries and remains the subject of ongoing research and debate. Its existence nonetheless demonstrates that the Neolithic transition in western Europe involved diverse pathways and a complex interplay between incoming farming populations and existing hunter-gatherer societies.
